name: Baker, William Addison
motto:
"Work? Am I not at work from morn till night?"
—*Biglow Papers*.
clubs: Member Instrumental Club, F., So., J., S.
description: The Editor in Chief had sent me to interview Bill Baker. This is what happened when I was ushered into his presence: He waved me to a vacant spot on the trunk, which I shared with a dress shirt (typifying Bill the fusser) and a gross of check books done up with wire (typifying Bill the universal manager) and a picture of the Physics Building (typifying Bill, Joe's pawn in the Student Council). "Pleased to see you—you've got one dollar and eight cents coming—if you don't cut out the low comedy at rehearsal it's the tall timbers for yours—yes, me and Joe Ames—I can swing the students and Joe can fix the faculty—certainly I'll take the job. What's it like?" I gasped feebly in reply, till suddenly I realized that his latter remarks were being aimed at both phones and a long distance. Meanwhile his right hand composed a "Resolution regarding Physics I," while his left constructed a poster: COTILLON. FEB. 12TH. COME. FISHER'S ORCHESTRA AND I WILL BE PRESENT. SEE ME AT ONCE. $2.50. WILLIAM A. BAKER. Suddenly glancing at his desk schedule he stopped. "7.13—breathing space." He breathed. Then opening for an instant a trunk he took out a dark brown bottle—he became beatified, and made a noise like a connoisseur. I glimpsed a card tied to the neck of the bottle. It read, "From Fritz and Whiff."

name: Moses Baroway
motto: "Moïse commence à moisir par les bords." —Boileau.
clubs: Hopkins Representative in Peace Contest, J.
description: MOSE is certainly a funny little fellow, especially when he gets all excited about his lessons—yes, he always calls them "my lessons." Everything has to be just so with him. He must map out his night's work, and do it or die. But have you ever seen any of the done work? It's a perfect mess. Mose buys a nickel's worth of theme paper, puts it in his brief-case and goes home to write "The Life and Work of Adam Smith." After copying all the available data he can find, he blots the paper, taking great care to smear the ink in as many directions as possible. He then ties a piece of string (red preferred, for red is his favorite color) to the papers and drags them up and down Pratt street for about an hour. If there is slush on the ground the refining process requires only fifteen minutes. The papers are then straightened out and pressed, being at this time ready to present to Doc. Whitney. Nevertheless, Mose is a good student of French, and, we'll swear to it, hell on Hebrew. His conferences with Doc Ember, and they're many and long, have brought him to a great state of perfection in the high-Yiddish tongue, and Mose can claim the distinction of being one of the authorities of his class, i. e., he has mastered a subject. Mose is a great smoker, a foul pipe in which he smokes dried striated muscles and ductless glands being his chief vice. This pipe and its burning tissues being pushed up Howard street by a little, stockily-built kamerad is all that is needed to announce his presence. You always see and smell the pipe first, and then you hear Mose.

name: Bartlett, Joseph Thomas
motto: "And ever honoured for his worthinesse." —Greene.
description: Despite his cherub countenance Joe is always ready for a "scrap." As soon as he joined us in the Sophomore year he declared war on the Freshmen and did valiant service in the many battles of that famous year. In fact, he lost a handful of his beautiful blonde hair when he "peeped" around a corner to see if the Freshmen were coming. It happened that a Freshman was also on the verge of peeping, but Joe got his head around the corner first and lost the hair. He got class numerals for the sacrifice.

name: Biser, Daniel Benton
motto:
"She is a winsome wee thing,
She is a handsome wee thing,
She is a bonny wee thing,
This sweet wee wife o' mine."
—Burns.
clubs: Librarian Glee and Instrumental Clubs, S.
Glee and Instrumental Clubs, So., J., S.
description: No, this delicate, passionate, supersensitive face is not that of Rabindranath Tagore, nor even of Alexander Geddes. Nor does it portray the true man, for beneath the oiled hair is encased one of the most fertile and interesting brains of the class. On looking into the eyes one would presuppose genius; in truth, Wyf (named after the late lamented Wyf of Bath), although being erratic enough, has not the originality to become a genius. When we say he lacks originality we mean that he does nothing new or nothing that he himself has thought out. Everything with him is in terms of Bill Baker. Indeed, they are constantly playing a little game called "Shadows," the object being for one always to stand on the shadow projected by the other. He eats, sleeps, studies, loafs and works with the image or material form of Bill constantly before him. They call themselves each other's wives, Bill being "Wifey," and Biser "Wyf." They have the same interests, the same likes and dislikes. They are both guilty of sitting in comfortable wicker chairs on balmy porches playing their mandolins for the delectation of dreamy-eyed admirers. That's the life for them! These moments are their bliss. They are ardent student lovers. It is a shame to drag Bill into this, to be sure, but how may we tell of Wyf without incriminating Wifey. And further incrimination:—We see them now, two "Schimmelreiters," shoving aside the swinging doors in their hunt for "sossiges" and the scintillating accompaniments thereof. Look at Wyf, but don't smile! Just recall those sweet, sad college boys immortalized in the play "Old Heidelberg"—those boys who fought duels, loved innkeepers' daughters, and who didn't work. Take two of the very best of them, and you have Bill and Biser—the one a diamond, and the other its platinum setting.

name: Wilmer Brinton, Jr.
motto: "True as the dial to the sun." —Butler's "Hudibras."
clubs: Captain Independent Water Polo Team, S.
description: I must ask you kindly to step back from the edges of the pool. Mr. Wilmer Brinton, Jr., is about to show you a fancy dive, and if he hits the water flat (as he usually does) the splash will cover a circle of one hundred yards radius. And so in a great many things he does there is a large splash. For instance, Ted Mercer, psychology, chemistry, the Poli Players, lunch at the Rennert, bridge, swimming, politics, Billy Sunday, Doc Andrews and B. V. D.—persons and things that arouse but casual interest in the rest of us—cause him to rise to great heights of enthusiasm and advocacy. It is always unwise to enter into conversation upon these matters unless you have an afternoon to waste away in the barber shop. "Jeff" is a relentless debater, and the only way discussions with him can be settled—(well, you know how). For some unaccountable reason every chair in which he sits begins to squeak. The only exception is the chair in Walter's salon. Here "Jeff" chose to make his headquarters, and without him there is no hospitality in the place. He keeps Walter in a good humor. One afternoon, there being nothing else to talk about, "Jeff" contracted to eat a 13-pound turkey on a $5 wager. If he survives this ordeal (but perhaps it will not be so difficult for one of such proportions) he will have added another superlative attribute to those which characterize him already. He will be the man of greatest capacity, as well as the heaviest, the noisiest, the least perturbed, the sportiest and the most affable member of the class.

name: Arthur Edward Brooks
high school: Franklin High School, of Reisterstown, Md.
motto: "At school I knew him—a sharp-witted youth, Grave, thoughtful and reserved among his mates, Turning the hours of sport and food to labor." —Old Play.
description: The claims of Teddy Prince and Doc Warren to the contrary notwithstanding, Brooks is the real Rube of our class. Being extremely proud of their rustic origin, he and Ebaugh have formed a Reisterstown Club at Hopkins, the chief duty of which is to extol the merits of that renowned institution of learning, the Franklin High School, of Reisterstown, Md. Brooks is a man after Rev. Spencer's own heart. He devotes so much time to his church duties out in the country that he never has time enough to prepare his lessons. Therefore, the extremely low marks he receives. The Reverend says that he doesn't believe it, but the Reverend is a "malicious person" and wishes to have a monopoly of saintliness. Brooks once dined with the Dean, being no exception to the common fate of seniors, and has never ceased to talk of the wonderful time he had, listening to George Hall instruct the Dean in matters of chemistry and physics. According to Brooks, the Dean showed a keen interest in the crops and kindred subjects—probably to escape Hall—and discoursed with him at great length upon the constituents of the soil of Baltimore county. This is why he was so sorry when the Dean began to fidget and he had to leave. But he says he will call again and finish that talk with the Dean.

name: Leopold Clarence Cohn
motto: "He sleeps by day more than the wildcat." —Shakespeare.
description: COHN'S massacre of the language of Lafayette distinguishes him from the rest of us. His greatest ambition is to speak French, but to date he is not even so proficient as Eiseman. Some members of the class, including Tarr, say that the impression he makes trying to talk French will be carried to their graves. Laying aside his pronunciation of French and his beautiful red complexion, the next thing worthy of note is that he is a motorcyclist. One day last summer a classmate hearing an altercation at the paying-teller's stand in the Commonwealth Bank stopped to find out what was the trouble. He saw—and great was the astonishment thereat—his friend Cohn, not dressed in the pink of style as is his wont, but clothed from top to bottom in a dirty, greasy, khaki suit with an enormous pair of horn goggles (even larger than Doc Dunlap's), trying to get money for a check. He had just been released on bail for speeding, and had come to get money to pay his fine. The teller couldn't recognize him, and Clarence was almost arrested the second time in the same day. Tarr says Cohn can dance better than Niles and Stargardter, which seems to imply that Tarr, too, attends these dances.

name: Cole, Raymond Kenmore
motto:
"Busy, busy he was; all the time
He spent in doing works to advance his station."
—Old Play.
description: COLE is a quiet little fellow, and very much like his nursery-rhyme ancestor who is a "merry old soul." When it came time to "write him up," Editor Brinton insisted that he be compared with his ancestor, so we must develop that topic. He wears always a pleasant smile—our word *always* including that period just preceding the final examination in Physics I. Cole, remarkable it is to relate, stood in the crowd of distressed students of Physics I waiting for the doors to open for examination *wearing a smile*. Everyone nearby was fatigued, gloomy and all had dark circles under their eyes. But Cole, calm, crammed and smiling went to the ordeal in happy humor. This fact impressed us—it described the man. There is a saying that you can always tell the temperament of a person while he is sitting at a game of bridge. We also think this may be said of persons waiting for James's finals. We tested Cole then; we have admired him since. He is a studious little fellow, and so unobtrusive that it has been the fortune of only a few to know him well. Like Bill Merrick, he too plays the "glad game," and his happiness and good-humor have made him a favorite.

name: Charles Everard Deems
motto: "Can no rest find me, no private place secure me, But still my miseries like bloodhounds haunt me?" —"Woman Pleased."
clubs: Manager of Varsity Track Team, S.
Secretary of Tenth Annual Fifth Regiment-Johns Hopkins Indoor Games Committee, S.
description: Yea, verily, the modern Diogenes, minus the lantern and tub. Apparently so disgusted with life, and all that therein is, Ev wears an intensely serious face from morning to night. Like Atlas, he is bearing a tremendous load, and cannot find a Hercules to rid him of it. He is manager of the track team, and his greatest efforts are bent toward finding a team which can be managed. Hence the countenance of woe. Yes, we must confess it; Ev is one of the ministerial crew, together with "Rev" Spencer, Ted Prince and "Benedict" Perkins. Still there may be hope for him if he finally succeeds in realizing that he will be judged by the company he keeps, said company being the aforementioned reverends. Judging from Ev's misanthropic bearing, one would not hesitate in saying that the fair sex makes no impression upon him. Wrong again! We know of several that make a decided impression. One of them told him that his classic features resemble those of a Greek God, of Apollo Belvedere, to be specific, without the engaging smile or the good looks. So this year Ev has been studying Greek to learn more about this Apollo fellow, but, being of the Spencerian type, we are afraid that mythology will fail to impress him. Never mind, Ev, we wager that she* still thinks the same whether Apollo was a Baptist or not. *Blanche.

name: De Witt, John
motto: "After him, fellows, drag him to the block." —"Measure for Measure."
description: As a philosopher Jack has borne the cardinal badge of courage. We will let him speak for himself. Only thus can we portray his chameleon-like character, now so naive and now so perplexing. "Love!" said DeWitt with that peculiar caressing inflection of the voice,— "love!—it is a materialization of a nondescript activity or enterprise of the heart. It is much like life—our spirit, you know." Here he leaned closer, asked for a cigarette, ran his left hand through his silky-golden hair, and once again narrowed his eyes to mere slits,—a sure sign of philosophical perorations. "Probably I'm a little too ingenious; let me illustrate. Take the wind, for example. We can't see it, but we know it is present, around us. We see its effects, its results. But the wind itself, it is indescribable—nondescript—perspicacious, eh?" Again he paused, and his voice was vibrant with emotion as he continued, "Oh! I'm in fine form this morning—I feel the maggots crawling in my brain—life is fine—f-i-n-e!" A bell rang. (Another quiz in Psychology in ten minutes.) "Jack," I said earnestly, "are you ready for the quiz this morning?" A look of unutterable disgust overspread his face. "Why do you descend to that level?" he wailed. "What matters anything when the mind is thrilled with such thought, and the brain soars off into infinite space?" The atmosphere was growing too rare for me. I turned to go, but a touch at my elbow brought me about. Jack was at my side. "Can you lend me a quarter for lunch?" he murmured. I barely had time to accommodate him when "the empty kettle" (Lederer) pranced in with a roar and bore him off.

name: Ebaugh, Franklin Gessler
motto: "Alas, poor chin! Many a wart is richer." —Shakespeare.
description: Somehow or other the word cute suggests itself when we come to think about writing a few words on Frank Ebaugh. It's hard to say just why; but perhaps the sheer boyishness of the subject is accountable for the suggestion. Frank is right droll and funny at times, especially when he is worried about something—physics, for instance. During the Reign of Terror (i.e., when we studied physics) he used to dart about like a wild antelope. He thought he didn't know anything, and, although he is no grind, he was greatly distressed. His distress was funny, for it was expressed by a regular cataract of invectives in that queer Reisterstown dialect which is at once delightful, and doubly so when coming from such a cute little fellow. The thing that brought him most into class fame was his escape from the Freshmen in his Sophomore year. He had been put in an empty dwelling and stripped of his clothes. He wanted to escape badly, and going to the window noticed a pair of pajamas hanging on the wash-line in the yard next door. Frank got a clothes-prop and stole the "nighties." He put them on, went out into the alley, and bargained to pay the driver of a grocery wagon $1 to take him home. Frank got home, dressed in a handsome new suit, and arrived at our banquet just in time to tell us the story of his thrilling capture, imprisonment and ingenious escape. His capacity as a student varies directly with the social whirl of Reisterstown. We hear from a most reliable source that Frank warms quite a few sofas in this country district, and also that he is famed as a wit wherever he keeps company. Being thus petted and admired, he has to devote quite a bit of time to the maintenance of his reputation, and this is done at the expense of Logic, Psychology and Physics, that trinity of intangibility.

name: Ford, Frank R.
motto: "I will now begin to sigh, read poets, look pale, go neatly, and be most apparently in love." —Marston.
description: FRANK is ardently fond of poetry, and his passion has led him to sit three years at the edge of Mother Greene's Parnassus enjoying her spicy English Literature courses. In fact, he even writes poetry; but none saving DeWitt and Gichner have been so fortunate as to break through that icy atmosphere which surrounds him and hear the verse. Frank is somewhat of a puzzle. No one knows his middle name; few know his habits, his likes, his dislikes. He hates convention; he advocates originality that borders on anarchy. He loves to bring stray dogs into McCoy Hall, and he loves to turn the pictures of Chaucer and the Canterbury Pilgrims to the wall. It is not fair to say he likes "rough house," for that is too undignified. Frank must be stern and unrelentless. His tricks and jokes must have sting, or he considers them child's play. During his first year he acquired the nickname "Sookey"—the name having been suggested while he stood bareheaded at sunset looking at a grazing cow. This was during one of his poetic, pastoral moods. But how quickly he changes humor. His friend (the one that coined the nickname) turned to him and called him "Sookey." Frank smiled, clenched his fist and fought. But there was no permanent anger; Frank soon "makes up." The incident, better than anything we know, is characteristic of the man. He's a staunch friend; a modern man made treasonable by too much classics and a love for Byron.

name: Hall, George Martin
motto: "It would talk; Lord, how it talked!" —Beaumont and Fletcher.
description: HALL? Yes, he's that short man with the pointed chin, the grandmother specs and the loud voice. Talks in the key of G with both feet on the loud pedal and all stops out. Yes, that's the man. Ask anyone about the University and they'll swear—yes, you'll have to stop them swearing—that they've heard of Hall. And yet even this quality must have its redeeming features, for behold the job Hall holds by virtue of it. Two years ago there was a dearth of material for the state roads commission. With a view to remedying conditions and filling the ranks of the summer road inspectors the Superintendent sent to Hopkins asking Dr. Magoffin, chief mogul of the student employment bureau, to pick out a student who, "regardless of other qualities, cannot comprehend an insult, is impervious to hints contrary to his own desires, and can vie with a circus 'spieler' in loquacity." There was only one man who could fill the bill, and that explains how it is that Hall is the "spieler" for the state roads commission, and why on every occasion, regardless of hints to desist and pseudo-insults, George rattles away about "my experiences on the State Roads, etc."

name: Joel Gutman David Hutzler
motto:
"An amber scent of odorous perfume
His harbinger." —Milton.
description: JOEL is one of the class's gentlemen, both in looks and in deportment. Neatly dressed, and always bowing and smiling, he has won for himself the Chesterfield honors of the class. The worst thing we have heard about him is a touching little account of his "heart-history," as Poe does say. Rumors have it that Joel is a dashing Lochinvar, using that big red limousine instead of a snorting horse. He has made an idol of some fair lady, who, 'tis said, considers herself too young yet to marry. Joel, pining away, drowns his sorrows in statistics, much to the delight of Eiseman and Baroway, who find his work an excellent "guide" for theirs. His name, a consolidation of department stores, comes in handy. He is called Joel on the dancing floors, in the basement of McCoy Hall, and at other festive assemblies. Dave he gets when he is hovering about his Irish companion, Steve Thompson. (Steve has to be original, you know.) And when he dresses in his pretty little red and gray gymnasium outfit to play basketball, he is called "Guttie," a nickname applied because he invariably strikes above the belt to rid his opponent of the ball. It is certainly a vulgar, vulgar name to use when referring to Joel, but the fact must be recorded as a matter of history. So pardon us!

name: Lederer, Lewis George
motto:
"Thou living ray of intellectual fire."
—Falconer's "Shipwreck."
(Don't take this seriously, though.)
description: LEW, Lewd, or Loud, is the empty kettle that makes the biggest noise. It has gotten so that whenever a boisterous laugh or an uncultured voice is heard around the University, no one looks for the source. It is simply understood to indicate the presence of this member of the Barber Shop School of Philosophy. It is generally thought that he seeks to make up for his lack of prominence in student activities by making himself noisily conspicuous. He poses as a deep student of heavy philosophy—as a disciple of von Treitschke and as the living intellect able to appreciate Kant, Schopenhauer and "Sex" Gichner. It is said that he once managed to stay out of bed until 4 A. M. reading so that he might mention the fact to us in an off-hand manner as though it were a nightly occurrence. The following is a sample of his conversation: "Ah-ha, boys, that reminds me of a thought in Dr. Loeb's 'Mechanistic Conceptions of Life.' That's a grand book. I was so interested in it that I sat up until 4 o'clock reading it." As a matter of fact no one suspects that he did. Poor Lew, Lewd, or Loud!

name: Levinson, Charles William
motto: "Meanwhile he smokes, and laughs at merry tales, Or pun ambiguous, or conundrum quaint." —John Phillips.
description: Our chemical-biological-geological highbrow! He's the indisputable authority in each. One day he came rushing breathlessly into the Chemical Laboratory to announce that he had just taken a civil-service examination for junior chemist. This is how he related the experience to a circle of blue-aproned colleagues: "Well, the exam was rather stiff, fellows, but it didn't stump me. You see, I know a lot about chemistry, physics, psychobiology, geology, anatomy, astronomy, celestial mechanics and osteopathy. I had to rely on my memory entirely, for I had no idea how to prepare for the exam"— This line of talk he kept up until the circle dropped away one by one, Doc Gilpin also leaving with a smile on his face. Levinson, may it be added, has a beautiful voice, and Mother Greene, recognizing the fact, repeatedly calls upon him to read "Paradise Lost" to the class. Milton, you know, was the "great God-gifted organ voice of England." Levi's isn't an organ voice; it more closely resembles a bassoon; and its pathetic, dulcet notes have been widely admired.

name: Merrick, William Spedden
motto: "Rantin', roarin' Willie." —Burns.
clubs: Member Independent Glee Club (am Fenster liegt dein Geld)
Varsity Track Squad, So., J., S.
description: THE truest humorist since Falstaff, Bill is a source of happiness to the whole class. His declensions of Spanish verbs, classes in barber-shop etymology and imitations of the Dean's lectures have brought him into eminent fame as a wit; and his singing of old ballads, a performance notable for peculiar discords, has made him so magnetic that a small court of admirers assembles daily to bask in his happiness. Bill is a male "Pollyanna," which means that he plays the "glad game." And while we are making these comparisons we may stop to record that he also resembles "Penrod," that naughty little boy who was brought up to tricks. You see, Bill will not hesitate to go to the locker of one of his rivals on the track team and put a mushy starfish that he has swiped from the biological lab into the folds of a beautiful blue silk shirt. The rival returns from the shower and soon plasters his shiny back with the starfish. Bill, meanwhile, is enjoying uproarious fits of laughter. He is the ray of sunshine to the track team, and is one of the few men able to amuse its stern coach. He loves cats and candy, and hence early in his Hopkins career found Doc Adams a genial friend. Doc loves to have Bill come in to stroke the backs of his many pets, and in return sells him a nickel's worth of candy for a penny. It is also rumored that Doc puts catnip in Bill's drinks instead of malted milk, and this is given as the reason for Bill's kittenishness. He appears to most advantage at the cotillons. His bright, shining face, his winning manner, his bewitching laugh and comical line of "small talk" making him very much in demand among the ladies. He is so fond of dancing that he tries to practice new steps with the Rev. Spencer and other serious-minded classmates. Indeed, someone has hinted that Bill longs to become a leader in Baltimore's society. Certainly he should succeed.

name: Nicholson, Norman Clive
motto: "Confidence is that feeling by which the mind embarks in great and honourable courses with a sure hope and trust in itself." —Cicero.
description: Nick has ideas of his own as to how the University should be run. He is sure he could conduct Chemistry II and Psychology so that the average student could learn at least a little about the subjects; and he feels that the Student Council is an absurdity which he alone is able to reconstruct into efficiency. This confidence in his own ability is apparent in everything he attempts. He is a firm believer in himself, and predicts a brilliant future. He is to study medicine, and if his success is proportionate to his success to date in dissecting cats, Baltimore is destined to have another Dr. William H. Welch (or do we mean another Dr. Leonard Keene Hirshberg). He still remembers the humiliation he suffered on the first day he came to the University. He was forced to take off his coat, vest and shoes and to run around the streets hunting for "next Tuesday." A crowd of upper classmen, much amused at his downcast spirit (for it was a great insult to one of such dignity), stood by and jeered. Nick never got over this. He is a mighty useful man to have in the class, for he is the bitter enemy of long lessons. If the instructor assigns more work than he can easily do in half an hour, he files a prompt and vigorous protest. Great sighs are heaved, and these, failing, are followed by groans of imposition. Kind-hearted men have given in to his moods, and they were rewarded with broad smiles of thanks that reached from pole to pole. He never lets himself be imposed upon, and is an economist of the midnight oil. During the summer he is a seed salesman "out West," and his experiences are the main topics of his conversations during the winter. Oh, how we have suffered!

name: Perkins, Alvin Thomas
motto: "The joys of marriage are the heaven on earth." —Ford.
description: Here's our wisest man. He got married before he got his A. B. Others, including Uhler and Tarr, tried to imitate Perk, but the Rev. Spencer refused to tie the knots for them until they paid their class dues. Perkins, to get serious again, is one of the ministers in the class, and being busy, thoughtful and studious, finds little time to mingle with his classmates. This has brought about a curious relationship between his classmates and himself. You see, he never appears at the barber shop, Brinton's office and the centre of Hopkins student activity, and is hence not very well known. Being thus somewhat of a stranger, and so very dignified, he has won great respect, and we feel on meeting him that the proper salutation should be "Good-morning, Reverend Perkins." But remembering that Reverend also applies to Spencer, we must not be disrespectful to Perkins, so we say simply, "Hello, Perk." When it came time to prepare this "write-up," we sent a spy to Perk's church to collect data. The spy reported that the Rev. thrilled his flock with highbrow political economy which had just been learned from Doc Hollander. It was said that he told them all about the theories of marginal utility and diminishing returns, not overlooking the compensated dollar and "goose-plucking" tax gathering. Surely, a minister with such learned sermons will succeed; this is our prophecy.

name: Edmund Howard Prince
motto: "His gait is like the antelope upon the Eastern hill." —*Old Play*.
description: Ted gets up at five o'clock in the morning (if the hired man does not fail to call him), milks the cows, studies a little Hebrew as an eye-opener, walks six miles to Towson to catch the trolley, and arrives in Baltimore bright and early at 9:10 A. M. just in time for Pop Vincent's lecture. His predominant characteristics are a shambling gait and a fat, red, beef-like neck. His method of walking gives the on-lookers-from-the-rear the impression that he is lame in both legs. Dr. Swartz, who, as we know, is *some* authority on walking, is still puzzled about Ted's adoption of amoeboidal movement. He remarked once that Ted went along like a *trilobite*, and consequently gave him an excellent mark in geology. (In fact, it was the most excellent mark Ted ever received—a 7.5).

name: Dorsey Richardson
motto: "His tawny beard was th' equal grace Both of his wisdom and his face." -Butler's "Hudibras."
clubs: Associate Editor The News-Letter, J., S.
description: DORSEY imagines that he is a highbrow. How he ever got this idea into his head we have not been able to find out. He corners some poor underclass man like Gichner and tells him that he ought to read Mill's Organon. He has a keen appetite for good literature, and can frequently be found reading the Cosmopolitan, Hearst's, and other such magazines. He has even gone so far as to write stories for a magazine, the name of which has been censored by the HULLABALOO BOARD. He is a great admirer of Kipling, having professed to have read all his works in a single night. As a traveler and man of letters he has few equals at Hopkins. When dining at the Dean's his main line of conversation was: "Out in California they did things in this way," or "the Westerns are so unlike the people of the Eastern Seaboard." Upon all occasions Dorsey tries to look intellectual. Whether it is in the barber shop, the classroom or the cage, his conversation smacks ever of learning. The greatest intellectual feat that he ever accomplished was applying for membership in the noble class of 1915. We have hopes that he will outgrow his learning, for, as Pope says, "A little learning is a dangerous thing."

name: Benjamin Sacks
motto: "Night after night / He sat and bleared his eyes with books." —Longfellow, "The Golden Legend."
description: BENNIE is a most credulous and gullible person—that is, outside of the classroom. Let any of his classmates tell him a most improbable yarn, or fill him full of "bull," clothed in words coined for the occasion, and Bennie swallows it whole, expressing a most lamentable desire for more. But in his lectures, however, he is different. It takes the most plausible and convincing arguments to turn him from his habitual unbelief, as poor old Doctor Dunlap has found. Not even the use of amusing whirligigs, bunches of bright-colored worsted, and unintelligible picture-slides could convince this youth that psychology is a useful and interesting study. He says that it is without a doubt "sensational," wherein the chief sensation is distress. We are afraid that if "Dunnie" hears him express such sentiments he will not get that 10 which he and Tarr covet so. Speaking of marks, we come to Bennie's chief grievance. He spends the few minutes when he is not studying in pouring into Stuart's ears his complaints about the iniquitous marking system at Hopkins, which compels the shining light of his intellectual gifts to remain hidden under a bushel of 9.5's. He says that he should have received a straight 10 in every course he took, but hard luck and malicious profs. alone prevented him. Poor boy! How Tarr sympathizes with him!

name: Rush Wiley Smith
motto: "He is moderation." —Fuller.
clubs: Usher, Hopkins-Fifth Regiment Games, J., S.
description: THE world take notice! Here's a man who can put an economic theory into practice. Rush Smith is a most persistent follower of the laissez-faire theory, which means that he don't give a d—m about anything that would cause him much worry. This also applies to logic, psychology and physics, three subjects which he thought he needn't follow very closely. They did not interest him much, so he applied laissez-faire. It's a most convenient theory to practice, and Smitty could do it with a conscience. The next point we take up is his keen joy in the theatre. He can tell you the life history of every actor or actress from Maude Adams to Billy Watson—a great range, you will observe. The theatre appeals to him as an antidote for dyspepsia, and being rather delicate, he seeks solace frequently. He also owns a comfortable automobile, called in these days a jitney, and having at his disposal such a pleasant means of entertainment, he gained great popularity from the start. Now don't imply that Smitty couldn't have made his friends without the car, for that is wrong. He is genial, a good listener, and laughs heartily (except upon the eve of examinations in Latin), and these attributes have won him a host of friends, and will continue to do so in times to come.

name: Harold Stewart
motto: "O lovely Polly Stewart, O charming Polly Stewart, There's ne'er a flower that blooms in May That's half so fair as thou art." —Burns.
clubs: Honorable Mention, F., J.; Special Scholarship, F.
description: He is the modest violet of the class, says Tom Troxell, who further describes him as the personification of bashfulness and timidity. He blushes upon the least provocation, which indicates innocence. He studies hard and long, and gets wonderful marks; but does not suggest the grind to the extent that Tarr does. He expects to study medicine and become a great surgeon. This puzzles us, for we fear that one so tender-hearted would not hurt a cat, much less a stiff. But we are told that Harold is at times stern and violent. He was this way once when some one "borrowed" his notes and didn't return them at the contracted time. Harold, without his notes, was furious, and, we are told, said darn it.

name: Emmette Rigdon Spencer
motto:
"Hear how he clears the point o' Faith
   Wi' rattlin' an' wi' thumpin';
Now meekly calm, now wild in wrath,
   He's stampin', an' he's jumpin'!" —Burns.
clubs: Class Treasurer F., So., J., S.
Member Varsity Track Squad, So., J., S.
Musical Clubs, F., So., J., S.
description: This earnest gentleman has been "working to beat hell" for four years. Our phrase is happily chosen, for besides being a full-fledged and accredited minister of the gospel he engages in a variety of other activities, any one of which would occupy the full time of the average man. The Right Rev. has been entrusted with the class moneys for four years, and no one except Ford and DeWitt has suspected him of fleecing the treasury. These say he puts our money in the missionary funds of his church. Bill Merrick, taking another point of view, says that the church collections just about cover the amount required to pay the expenses of shaving the Right Rev. These items are constantly on the mind of the rev.: "What shall be the text for my next sermon?" "Is my voice (as a member of the Glee Club) widely admired?" "Will I ever be able to do a mile run in ten minutes?" "How much money can I make as a notary of the public?" "Why won't Mother Greene ever pass me in Lit I?" "Where can I turn for another ad. for the HULLABALOO?" "Who ever started to call me 'rev.'?" "Wyde was his parrish and houses far asonder, But he nay lafte not for rain nor thonder."

name: Short, John Saulsbury
motto: "He has done the work of a true man; Crown him, honor him." —Whittier-Merrick. "He'd bear a pound weight any day to save anybody else carrying an ounce." —George Eliot.
clubs: Treasurer University Young Men's Christian Association, So.
Class Numerals in Football and Track, F., So.
Chairman Banquet Committee, So., J., S.
Correspondent for The Sun and The Evening Sun, So., J., S.
description: He is 6 foot. At an early age he showed ability in the literary line, and soon secured a position on the staff of the Johns Hopkins Gazette, a paper given over to athletic and theatrical news. Gaining the confidence of Owner Brinton, he soon arose to the position of Editor-in-Chief. He wrote under the pseudonym of "Hoy," and it is by this name that he is universally known. Not being satisfied with his early work, he determined to give Hopkins a place in the literary world. This he did by publishing three volumes entitled "Dope on English Literature, 1, 2 & 3." He is known to be a lover of music, and numbers among his friends Scotti. He next gained prominence by becoming manager of baseball. In this new position he loved to show his authority by taking two pieces of chewing gum. The press has commented on him as follows: "Dr. Hoy is well versed in literature, music, art, politics, managing, making banquet menus and cutting classes, and has striven during his career at Hopkins to help give Sidney Lanier 'a place in the sun.'" His brilliance was noticed by the HULLABALOO BOARD, and he was engaged at an enormous salary to be its Editor-in-Chief.

name: Swartz, Joel Howard
motto: "He was in logic a great critic, Profoundly skilled in analytic." —Butler's "Hudibras."
description: "Big he was of brawn and eke of bones." This applies to Joel, our overgrown young rock-breaker. Anyone who expects to roughhouse Joel will come out a sadder and a wiser man. Being still quite a good-natured kid, Joel does not hesitate to bestow on any classmate a resounding thwack on the shoulders with his great paw. Even psychology cannot erase from his face that ubiquitous, good-natured smile. It is just as characteristic of him as his down-covered upper lip, which, when the sun is shining, reminds one of a waving field of golden grain. To judge from the size of his feet, he would make a good policeman, and he ought to succeed as a geologist, a profession in which the chief requirements seem to be big feet and a long stride. Joel is an embryo geologist. As a chemist and a biologist he is also very promising, but the darling of his thoughts, the idol of his dreams, is psychology, a science which he adores for the practice it affords his analytical mind.

name: Tarr, Frederick Courtney
motto:
"Up! Up! my Friend, and quit your books,
Or surely you'll grow double;
Up! Up! my Friend, and clear your looks,
Why all this toil and trouble?"
—Wordsworth.
clubs: Hopkins Scholar F., So., J., S.
description: COURTNEY is one of those individuals who is bubbling over with information. He is intellectually irrepressible. That is why he has been unable to put aside his prep-school habit of holding up his hand in classroom when he wants to show knowledge of psychobiology. He studies harder than Weech or Brinton, and yet claims he is not a grind. We all know, however, that failure to receive a 10 in every course would break his heart. The English language soon became inadequate for him to express his many opinions, so he turned to French and Spanish. In fact, he has become so attached to the tango and other things Spanish that almost all of his spare time is spent in some sort of Spanish diversion. Every fortnight he is discovered attending a "fandango"* with a most captivating "senorita." Just let anyone say a word or two in French or Spanish and see how quickly he comes over to hear (and comment upon or correct) it. Tarr is a steady and companionable fellow, being at times very witty and bright. So long as his mind may be diverted from 10's and Spanish he's great company; but when that volley of curses is brought down upon the course that fails to provide the "god-10",—from this preserve us. Ah, that he would say these things in Spanish to double and yet to obscure the misery.

name: Stephen Ignatius Thompson
motto:
"Born for success, he seemed
With grace to win, with heart to hold,
With shining gifts that took all eyes." —Emerson.
description: THE editors waited for St. Patrick's Day to invoke the muse to immortalize Steve, the happy, easy-going Irishman of the class. Never impulsive, never violent; always faithful, always sympathetic. The shout goes up: "Long live the Irish." We chuckle when we think of Steve. Here is how an admirer has described him: "He is of a very calm and steady nature: nothing ever disturbs him. The weather alone has power over him. He changes inversely as the rise and fall of the temperature. He seldom works, trusting always to luck, his best friend. Before any student crisis he may be seen exchanging wishes of good luck with his fellow sufferers." Sometimes, however, luck has failed him, but being Irish, which also means being resourceful, he has employed successful strategy. For instance, he knows that Doc Matthews bases his marks on the ability of his students to answer his oral quizzes. Steve could not hold a note-book open during these quizzes, so he could not get good marks. Enter strategy! Steve scratched notes with a pin on the surface of the table at which he sat. He was then able to star at quizzes. Gazing thoughtfully at his table he could soon find the correct answer to the Doc's questions. Result: 10. Method: strategy a substitute for luck. And he'll go through life in just such a manner. We can't predict his future, but we'll bet he'll be prosperous and happy. Why shouldn't he? A man as solid as the tread of time!

name: Troxell, Thomas Franklin
motto: "Some popular chief, More noisy than the rest; but cries Halloa! And in a trice the bellowing herd come out." —Dryden.
clubs: Varsity Lacrosse Team, F., So., J., S.
description: THOMAS FRANKLIN TROXELL is a peroxide blonde, whose soft, silken tresses house the intellect of one of the greatest men at Hopkins (in his own opinion). Tommy’s sleepy look is caused by the deep-sunken eyes and the light thistle-down which serves as eyebrows. When he was a little fellow he used to run about Walbrook chasing butterflies with a net. This was an early training for lacrosse, a game at which he has lately become quite famous. He is known at Lehigh as the guy that broke Red’s rib; at Swarthmore as the fellow who cut off Sweeny’s ear; at Cornell, the man who shaved the goal-keeper, and at Hopkins as the foster-child of Bill Schmeisser. He is fond of showing his authority as captain of the lacrosse team, and his voice calling “shoot,” “cover up,” etc., rings out in the cage in headache-producing loudness. As a civilian his delight is in “kidding” DeWitt and Lederer. Nicholson has been the only one so far to hypnotize him into any kind of submission. He has been mentioned many times as a possibility for the Deanship, but he will fail to qualify because this office calls for a dark-haired man who is not familiar with athletics.

name: Uhler, Claude Irving
motto: "For I am not the first who found the name of Mary fatal." —Meredith.
clubs: Class Numerals in Football, Track, Baseball, Lacrosse, Glee Club, F., So., J. S.
description: UHLER is not a ladies' man,—that would be far too trite a manner of expressing it. Claude is a TAR BABY with the ladies. "Once met forever kept" is his slogan, and 'tis true that Claude never strikes a name from the long list of his fair acquaintances. Alas! his whole life is predominated by one idea—the ladies. Observe how adroitly he has managed to make even his college work a means for further feminine triumphs. The windows of the Biological Laboratory are strategically situated for his purpose. Well he knows that every afternoon at three a procession of the most charming of the Western High School girls are due to pass these windows. With characteristic cunning he has selected this year the second window from the end, where he can be the first to descry the approach of the fair and the last to bid them—with those bold, avid eyes of his—to tarry and smile. And observe with what diabolic foresight he has pursued advanced courses in German. He knows full well what a dearth of men and a plentitude of rich widows Germany will offer for his gay advances. It need hardly be mentioned that he has been a hero on the football field. Nothing could place him more favorably in the eyes of the ladies than a football injury, and it naturally follows that Claude was injured. Bandages, crutches, distressed look—he wore them all. Oh, Claudius, when will you marry?

name: Voshell, Allen Fiske
motto: "He was so generally civil that nobody thanked him for it." — Sam Johnson.
clubs: Class Football, Baseball, Track and Lacrosse teams
description: Finicky, fastidious, bossy, care free, over-polite, inclined to bull-headedness, full of pretty parlor tricks, a healthful animal and as a result athletic, a good lady-flatterer — there you have "Al," known to his intimates as "Mother Voshell."

name: Warren, Avra Melvin
motto: "He could on either side dispute, Confute, change hands, and still confute." —Butler's "Hudibras."
clubs: Y. M. C. A. Council, S.
description: Warren, ever since we have known him, has been in love, lazy and loquacious. On the first day he appeared at Hopkins a very pretty little girl came around at five o'clock after laboratory and, after an affectionate greeting, the pair left the vicinity arm in arm. She has returned many times since for her "doctor love," but none of us has ever been able to get deep into the facts of the case. The couple comes and goes, a beautiful spectacle—"all the world loves a lover." Furthermore, Warren is lazy. He is dreamy, and gazes at the sky on pretty days. This constantly distracts his mind from all other work, yet he manages to maintain quite a high reputation as a student. This reputation we believe he has built up by his rather original habit of carrying on debates in the class room with the professors. "Doc" has done some work as a debater, and has become possessed of the idea that he has wonderful talent. During lectures he seizes every opportunity to take an opposite view of the subject under discussion. He then proceeds to hold forth for about fifteen minutes on what he believes and why. Of course, professors are happy at being relieved of their work, and their gratitude is expressed on the report card. He is very fond of Mother Greene and MacKenzie's buns, and has many other little peculiarities.

name: Charles Sewell Weech
motto: "For Rhetoric he could not ope His mouth, but out there flew a trope." —Butler's "Hudibras."
clubs: Member Intercollegiate Debating Teams, J. S.
description: At college his career, of course, was highly creditable. And here he prepared himself for public life, into which he was to be introduced by the patronage of his uncle, Lord Binkie, by studying the ancient and modern orators with great assiduity, and by speaking unceasingly at the debating societies. But though he had a fine flux of words, and delivered his little voice with great pomposity and pleasure to himself, and never advanced any sentiment or opinion which was not perfectly trite and stale and supported by a Latin quotation, yet he" . . . . . . . . . verbatim from Thackeray. As an orator there are few like him in the University. He puffs, stamps, blows, rants, lopes, prances, beats his chest and is finally told by the chairman that he has "overspoken" his time by an hour. Whereupon Weech bawls "Y-a-a-s" and is led frowning and scowling to his seat by the halfbacks of the debating team. Before going to press we learn that he is to make the principal speech at the opening of the new gas plant at Homewood. His subject will be, "Why I know as much, if not more, than Tarr." One of his chief delights is trying to rattle Ed Griffin. Ed, on these occasions, takes him by the horns and easily exposes his fallacies.
